name: VCF Bring-up: Host Reported Unresponsive — DNS PTR Record Missing
description: VCF bring-up reports an ESXi host as unresponsive during validation. Host is perfectly reachable by IP. Root cause is a missing PTR record — bring-up validates reverse DNS for all components.
customerScenario: The VCF bring-up wizard is saying one of our ESXi hosts is unresponsive. But we can ping it, we can SSH to it, vCenter can see it. Everything looks fine from our end. The bring-up is the only thing that thinks there's a problem.
customerFollowUp: We set up DNS for all the hosts before we started. Forward lookups work — we checked. We didn't specifically check reverse lookups though.
fixSteps: Test reverse DNS lookup for host IP from SDDC Manager
Confirm PTR record is missing in DNS
Add PTR record in DNS server
hints: When bring-up reports a host as unresponsive but you can reach it by IP and FQDN, what does that tell you about where the validation is failing? What does bring-up check beyond basic connectivity?

VCF bring-up validates both forward and reverse DNS for every component. Try resolving the host by FQDN — does it return the correct IP? Then try reverse lookup of that IP — does it return the correct FQDN?
Run nslookup on the host's IP address from SDDC Manager. Does it return the expected FQDN or does it fail?

The reverse DNS lookup for the host's IP is failing — there is no PTR record in DNS. Bring-up validates PTR records for all components and treats a missing PTR as the host being unresponsive, which is misleading.

Add the missing PTR record to your DNS server for the affected host's IP address. Verify the reverse lookup works from SDDC Manager using nslookup. Re-run bring-up validation — the host should now pass.
author: Jon — CloudITBlog.com
